items.name: is_absolute
items.doc: Returns `true` if `p` starts with `/`; the empty path is not absolute.

items.name: is_relative
items.doc: Returns `true` if `p` is not absolute.

items.name: basename
items.doc:
Returns the last path component as a new `String`, ignoring trailing slashes.
`"a/b/c"` → `"c"`, `"/foo"` → `"foo"`, `"foo/"` → `"foo"`, `"/"` → `"/"`, `""` → `""`.

items.name: dirname
items.doc:
Returns the directory portion of `p` as a new `String`, ignoring trailing slashes.
`"a/b/c"` → `"a/b"`, `"/foo/bar"` → `"/foo"`, `"foo"` → `"."`, `"/"` → `"/"`, `""` → `"."`.

items.name: extension
items.doc:
Returns the file extension of the last component including the dot, or `""` if none.
`"foo.txt"` → `".txt"`, `"archive.tar.gz"` → `".gz"`, `"Makefile"` → `""`; dotfiles such as
`".bashrc"` have no extension.

items.name: stem
items.doc:
Returns the basename of `p` without its extension.
`"foo.txt"` → `"foo"`, `"archive.tar.gz"` → `"archive.tar"`, `".bashrc"` → `".bashrc"`.

items.name: join
items.doc:
Joins two path components with a single `/`, returning a new `String`.
`join("a/b", "c")` → `"a/b/c"`, `join("a/b/", "c")` → `"a/b/c"`.
If `b` is absolute it replaces `a` (POSIX semantics).

items.name: join_into
items.doc:
Appends `component` to `base` in place, inserting a `/` separator if needed.
If `component` is absolute it replaces `base` entirely (POSIX semantics).

items.name: normalize
items.doc:
Normalizes `p` lexically: collapses `//`, drops `.` segments, resolves `..` (no FS access).
Matches Go `path.Clean` / Python `posixpath.normpath`: `..` cannot escape root on absolute
paths but is preserved on relative ones (`"/../a"` → `"/a"`, `"../a/../b"` → `"../b"`);
`""` → `"."`; a trailing `/` is dropped unless the result is exactly `"/"`.
Segment tracking is capped at 256 deep; `..` may mis-resolve beyond that.

items.name: io_err_errno
items.doc: Collapses an `IoUringError` to a negative-errno `i32`; `RingFull` maps to `-EAGAIN` (-11).

items.name: new
items.doc:
Creates an `IoRing` with `entries` SQ/CQ slots.
On kernel init failure returns `Errno` carrying the negative errno.

items.name: finish
items.signature: fn finish(self: &mut IoRing, sqe: *mut u8) -> Result

items.name: read
items.signature: fn read(self: &mut IoRing, fd: i32, buf: *mut u8, len: i64, offset: i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Reads up to `len` bytes from `fd` into `buf` at file offset `offset`; returns bytes read.
Pass `offset = -1` to use the file's current position.

items.name: write
items.signature: fn write(self: &mut IoRing, fd: i32, buf: *const u8, len: i64, offset: i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Writes up to `len` bytes from `buf` to `fd` at file offset `offset`.
Returns the number of bytes written.

items.name: openat
items.signature: fn openat(self: &mut IoRing, dirfd: i32, path: *const u8, flags: i32, mode: i32) -> Result
items.doc:
Opens `path` relative to `dirfd` and returns the new fd; `AT_FDCWD()` (-100) resolves
relative to the cwd.
Common `flags` (fcntl.h, Linux x86-64): `O_RDONLY`=0, `O_WRONLY`=1, `O_RDWR`=2,
`O_CREAT`=64, `O_TRUNC`=512, `O_APPEND`=1024.

items.name: close
items.signature: fn close(self: &mut IoRing, fd: i32) -> Result
items.doc: Closes file descriptor `fd`.

items.name: O_RDONLY
items.doc: Returns the `O_RDONLY` flag (0): open read-only.

items.name: O_WRONLY
items.doc: Returns the `O_WRONLY` flag (1): open write-only.

items.name: O_RDWR
items.doc: Returns the `O_RDWR` flag (2): open read-write.

items.name: O_CREAT
items.doc: Returns the `O_CREAT` flag (64): create the file if it does not exist.

items.name: O_TRUNC
items.doc: Returns the `O_TRUNC` flag (512): truncate to length 0 on open.

items.name: O_APPEND
items.doc: Returns the `O_APPEND` flag (1024): append on every write.

items.name: O_NONBLOCK
items.doc: Returns the `O_NONBLOCK` flag (2048): open in non-blocking mode.

items.name: AT_FDCWD
items.doc: Returns `AT_FDCWD` (-100): resolve `openat` paths relative to the current directory.

items.name: exists
items.doc:
Returns `true` iff `path` exists (`lstat`-based: a dangling symlink counts as existing).
Errors that look like not-found (ENOENT, ENOTDIR) read as `false`; use `exists_checked`
to distinguish real I/O errors.

items.name: exists_checked
items.doc: Returns `Ok(true)`/`Ok(false)` for exists/not-exists, `Err(-errno)` for I/O errors.

items.name: is_dir
items.doc: Returns `true` iff `path` is a directory; follows symlinks, errors read as `false`.

items.name: is_file
items.doc: Returns `true` iff `path` is a regular file; follows symlinks, errors read as `false`.

items.name: exists
items.doc: Returns `true` iff `path` exists; safe `str` form (errors read as `false`).

items.name: is_dir
items.doc: Returns `true` iff `path` is a directory; safe `str` form (errors read as `false`).

items.name: is_file
items.doc: Returns `true` iff `path` is a regular file; safe `str` form (errors read as `false`).

items.name: file_size
items.doc: Returns the file size in bytes, or `Err(-errno)`.

items.name: mtime_ns
items.doc: Returns the modification time in nanoseconds since epoch, or `Err(-errno)`.

items.name: mtime_ns
items.doc: Returns the modification time in nanoseconds since epoch, or `Err(-errno)`; safe `str` form.

items.name: parent
items.doc:
Returns the directory part of `path`: everything before the last `'/'`.
Borrowed slice into `path` (no copy); `""` if `path` has no `'/'`.

items.name: mkdir
items.signature: fn mkdir(path: *const u8, mode: i32) -> Result
items.doc: Creates directory `path` with permissions `mode`; `Err(-errno)` on failure, EEXIST included.

items.name: mkdir_p
items.doc:
Creates `path` and each missing directory along it with mode 0o755 (`mkdir -p`).
Existing directories are not an error; a non-directory non-leaf component yields
`Err(-ENOTDIR)`.

items.name: rmdir
items.doc: Removes the empty directory `path`, or returns `Err(-errno)`.

items.name: unlink
items.doc: Removes (unlinks) the file at `path`, or returns `Err(-errno)`.

items.name: unlink
items.doc: Removes (unlinks) the file at `path`, or returns `Err(-errno)`; safe `str` form.

items.name: rm_rf
items.doc:
Removes `path` and everything under it (`rm -rf` semantics); returns the first error hit.
A non-existent path is not an error. The directory check follows symlinks, so a symlink
to a directory is traversed and the target's contents are removed.

items.name: filter
items.signature: fn filter(self: ReadDir, pred: fn(DirEntry) -> bool) -> FilterIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: map
items.signature: fn map(self: ReadDir, f: MapFn) -> MapIter<ReadDir, DirEntry, MapOut, MapFn>

items.name: take
items.signature: fn take(self: ReadDir, n: i64) -> TakeIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: skip
items.signature: fn skip(self: ReadDir, n: i64) -> SkipIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: chain
items.signature: fn chain(self: ReadDir, other: ChainOther) -> ChainIter<ReadDir, ChainOther, DirEntry>

items.name: zip
items.signature: fn zip(self: ReadDir, other: ZipOther) -> ZipIter<ReadDir, ZipOther, DirEntry, ZipOtherItem>

items.name: min_by
items.signature: fn min_by(self: ReadDir, cmp: fn(DirEntry, DirEntry) -> i32) -> Option

items.name: max_by
items.signature: fn max_by(self: ReadDir, cmp: fn(DirEntry, DirEntry) -> i32) -> Option

items.name: take_while
items.signature: fn take_while(self: ReadDir, pred: fn(DirEntry) -> bool, zero: DirEntry) -> TakeWhileIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: skip_while
items.signature: fn skip_while(self: ReadDir, pred: fn(DirEntry) -> bool, zero: DirEntry) -> SkipWhileIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: step_by
items.signature: fn step_by(self: ReadDir, step: i64, zero: DirEntry) -> StepByIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: inspect
items.signature: fn inspect(self: ReadDir, visit: fn(DirEntry) -> void, zero: DirEntry) -> InspectIter<ReadDir, DirEntry>

items.name: fold
items.signature: fn fold(self: ReadDir, init: Acc, f: FoldFn) -> Acc

items.name: try_fold
items.signature: fn try_fold(self: ReadDir, init: Acc, f: TryFoldFn) -> ControlFlow

items.name: find_map
items.signature: fn find_map(self: ReadDir, f: FindMapFn) -> Option

items.name: reduce
items.signature: fn reduce(self: ReadDir, f: ReduceFn) -> Option

items.name: for_each
items.signature: fn for_each(self: ReadDir, f: EachFn) -> void

items.name: try_for_each
items.signature: fn try_for_each(self: ReadDir, f: TryEachFn) -> ControlFlow

items.name: any
items.signature: fn any(self: ReadDir, pred: AnyFn) -> bool

items.name: all
items.signature: fn all(self: ReadDir, pred: AllFn) -> bool

items.name: nth
items.signature: fn nth(self: &mut ReadDir, n: i64) -> Option

items.name: find
items.signature: fn find(self: &mut ReadDir, pred: FindFn) -> Option

items.name: position
items.signature: fn position(self: &mut ReadDir, pred: PosFn) -> Option

items.name: read_dir
items.doc:
Opens directory `path` for non-recursive iteration.
Returns `Err(-1)` on any open failure (errno is not propagated by `opendir`).

items.name: walk_dir
items.doc:
Returns the eager pre-order list of all entries rooted at `path`.
The first entry is `path` itself; each entry's `name` holds the full path, directly
usable in FS ops. Skips `"."`/`".."`. Directory checks are `stat`-based, so symlinks
to directories are traversed.

items.name: canonical
items.doc:
Resolves symlinks and returns an absolute, normalized path (wraps libc `realpath`).
Returns `Err(-errno)` on failure.

items.name: is_open
items.doc: Returns `true` if the handle holds a valid file descriptor (not yet closed).

items.name: read_at
items.signature: fn read_at(self: &mut File, buf: *mut u8, len: i64, offset: i64) -> i32
items.doc:
Reads up to `len` bytes at absolute `offset` into `buf`.
Returns bytes read (0 = EOF) or a negative errno on failure.

items.name: write_at
items.signature: fn write_at(self: &mut File, buf: *const u8, len: i64, offset: i64) -> i32
items.doc:
Writes `len` bytes from `buf` at absolute `offset`.
Returns bytes written (may be short) or a negative errno on failure.

items.name: close
items.doc:
Closes the file descriptor and marks the handle closed; idempotent.
Returns 0 on success (or if already closed), a negative errno on failure.

items.name: ring_open
items.signature: fn ring_open(path: *const u8, flags: i32, mode: i32) -> Result

items.name: open
items.doc: Opens NUL-terminated `path` read-only (`O_RDONLY`); `Err` carries a negative errno.

items.name: create
items.doc: Creates or truncates NUL-terminated `path` (`O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC`, mode 0666).

items.name: open_append
items.doc: Opens NUL-terminated `path` for append (`O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_APPEND`, mode 0666).

items.name: read_to_string
items.doc:
Reads the entire file at NUL-terminated `path` into a `String` (4 KiB chunks until EOF).
No UTF-8 validation; bytes are appended verbatim. `Err` carries a negative errno.

items.name: read_to_string
items.doc: Reads the entire file into a `String`; safe `str` overload that null-terminates `path`.

items.name: write_string
items.doc:
Creates or truncates the file at NUL-terminated `path`, then writes `s` with one `write`.
Returns bytes written; a short write is possible. `Err` carries a negative errno.

items.name: write_string
items.doc: Overwrites the file with `s`; safe `str` overload that null-terminates `path`.

items.name: url_encode_component
items.signature: fn url_encode_component(src: *const u8, len: i64, out: &mut String) -> void
items.doc:
Percent-encodes bytes `[src, src+len)`, appending to `out`.
Bytes outside the RFC 3986 unreserved set (`A-Za-z0-9-._~`) become uppercase `%XX`.

items.name: url_encode_component_str
items.doc: Percent-encodes `src`, appending to `out` (see `url_encode_component`).

items.name: url_encode_query_form
items.signature: fn url_encode_query_form(src: *const u8, len: i64, out: &mut String) -> void
items.doc:
Encodes bytes `[src, src+len)` as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, appending to `out`.
Same as `url_encode_component` except space (0x20) becomes `+`.

items.name: url_encode_query_form_str
items.doc: Form-urlencodes `src`, appending to `out` (see `url_encode_query_form`).

items.name: url_decode
items.signature: fn url_decode(src: *const u8, len: i64, out: &mut String, plus_is_space: bool) -> i64
items.doc:
Decodes percent-encoded bytes `[src, src+len)`, appending to `out`.
If `plus_is_space` is true, `+` decodes to a space (form-urlencoded mode).
Returns the number of bytes appended, or -1 on malformed input (truncated `%`
escape, non-hex digit); on error `out` may already hold partially decoded bytes.

items.name: url_decode_str
items.doc:
Decodes percent-encoded `src` into `out`; returns bytes appended, or -1 on malformed input.
`+` passes through unchanged (component mode).

items.name: url_decode_form_str
items.doc:
Decodes form-urlencoded `src` into `out`, with `+` decoded as space.
Returns the number of bytes appended, or -1 on malformed input.

items.name: find_byte
items.signature: fn find_byte(ptr: *const u8, len: i64, b: u8, from: i64) -> i64

items.name: matches_scheme_sep
items.signature: fn matches_scheme_sep(ptr: *const u8, len: i64, i: i64) -> bool

items.name: push_range
items.signature: fn push_range(dst: &mut String, ptr: *const u8, lo: i64, hi: i64) -> void

items.name: parse_port_digits
items.signature: fn parse_port_digits(ptr: *const u8, lo: i64, hi: i64) -> i32

items.name: url_parse
items.doc:
Parses an absolute URL of the form `scheme://host[:port][path][?query][#fragment]`.
Returns `None` on malformed input (bad scheme, empty host, non-numeric or >65535 port).
The fragment is parsed off and discarded; components are not percent-decoded.

items.name: url_default_port_s
items.doc: Returns the default port for a well-known scheme (`http` = 80, `https` = 443), or -1 if unknown.

items.name: url_format
items.doc:
Serializes `u`, appending to `out`.
Port is omitted when -1 or equal to the scheme's default; `?` appears only when
`query` is non-empty.  Path is emitted verbatim — caller supplies the leading `/`.

items.name: fls_slots
items.doc: Returns the number of fiber-local storage slots per fiber.

items.name: fls_key_cqe_result
items.doc: Returns the FLS key reserved for the fiber reactor's CQE result (slot 0).

items.name: sched_enqueue
items.signature: fn sched_enqueue(s: *mut Scheduler, fptr: i64) -> void

items.name: stack_pool_push
items.signature: fn stack_pool_push(s: *mut Scheduler, base: *mut u8) -> bool

items.name: retire_fiber
items.signature: fn retire_fiber(s: *mut Scheduler, dead: *mut Fiber) -> void

items.name: sched_new
items.doc:
Allocates and initialises a new scheduler.
The caller owns it and must call `sched_free()` when done.

items.name: sched_set_current
items.doc:
Registers this scheduler as the current one for this thread.
Must be called before `spawn()` or `yield_fiber()` (`sched_run` sets it itself).

items.name: sched_set_stack_pool_cap
items.signature: fn sched_set_stack_pool_cap(s: *mut Scheduler, cap: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Enables (or resizes) the retired-stack pool; `cap = 0` disables pooling.
Call after `sched_new()` and before the first `spawn()`.  Existing pool
contents are munmap'd before the slot buffer is replaced.

items.name: sched_free
items.doc: Frees the scheduler, munmapping any pooled stacks (does NOT free live fibers).

items.name: spawn
items.signature: fn spawn(f: fn(i64) -> void, arg: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Spawns a fiber that will call `f(arg)` on its own stack and enqueues it as ready.
The current scheduler must have been set (`sched_set_current` or `sched_run`).

items.name: yield_fiber
items.doc:
Yields voluntarily: re-enqueues the current fiber and switches to the scheduler.
Returns when the scheduler picks this fiber again.

items.name: logos_fiber_done
items.doc:
Terminates the current fiber: marks it for freeing and switches back to the
scheduler context; never returns.
Called by the `logos_fiber_entry` trampoline (`fiber_ctx.S`) after the fiber
function returns.

items.name: sched_run
items.doc:
Runs the scheduler loop until the ready queue is empty.
Sets the per-thread scheduler pointer so `spawn()` and `yield_fiber()` work.

items.name: fls_get
items.doc:
Returns the current fiber's FLS value for `key`, or 0 when no fiber is running.
`key` is not bounds-checked; valid range is `0..fls_slots()`.

items.name: fls_set
items.signature: fn fls_set(key: i64, val: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Sets the current fiber's FLS value for `key`; no-op when no fiber is running.
`key` is not bounds-checked; valid range is `0..fls_slots()`.

items.name: park_fiber
items.doc:
Parks the current fiber: like `yield_fiber()` but WITHOUT re-enqueueing it.
The caller must arrange re-enqueueing later (e.g. `unpark_fiber` from a
reactor CQE handler).  Returns when the fiber is resumed; no-op when no
fiber is running.

items.name: current_fiber
items.doc:
Returns a pointer-sized identifier of the current fiber, or 0 if none.
The reactor stores this as io_uring SQE `user_data` to know which fiber to
re-enqueue on CQE delivery.

items.name: unpark_fiber
items.doc:
Re-enqueues a previously-parked fiber into the ready queue.
Called by the reactor when a CQE for a parked fiber arrives; no-op when no
scheduler is set.

items.name: future_slot_new
items.doc: Allocates a new `FutureSlot<T>`, shared between the producer and consumer.

items.name: future_complete
items.signature: fn future_complete(slot: *mut FutureSlot<T>, val: T) -> void
items.doc:
Stores `val` into `slot` and wakes its waiting consumer, if any.
Wakes cross-thread via `cross_thread_enqueue` when the waiter lives on another thread.

items.name: future_get
items.doc:
Returns the value from `slot`, suspending the current fiber until it is ready.
Caller is responsible for freeing `slot` itself after `future_get` returns.

items.name: reactor_new
items.doc:
Allocates and initialises a new reactor with `entries` SQ/CQ slots.
Returns `0` (null) on `io_uring` init failure.

items.name: reactor_free
items.doc: Tears down the reactor and frees its allocations; no-op on null `r`.

items.name: reactor_set_current
items.doc: Installs `r` as the current thread's reactor (asm TLS slot).

items.name: reactor_current
items.doc: Returns the current thread's reactor, or `0` if none is installed.

items.name: reactor_has_parked
items.doc: Returns `true` if any fibers are parked awaiting CQEs; `false` for a null `r`.

items.name: reactor_get_sqe
items.doc:
Obtains a free SQE from the reactor's ring; returns `0` if the ring is full.
Caller must fill the SQE via a `logos_io_uring_prep_*` function before
`reactor_submit_and_park`.

items.name: reactor_submit_and_park
items.signature: fn reactor_submit_and_park(r: *mut Reactor, sqe: *mut u8) -> i32

items.name: reactor_poll
items.signature: fn reactor_poll(r: *mut Reactor, blocking: bool) -> i32
items.doc:
Drains completions from the ring, re-enqueuing each CQE's fiber with the result in its FLS[0].
If `blocking`, waits (unconditionally) for the first CQE, then drains the rest non-blocking.
Returns the number of CQEs processed, or a negative errno from the blocking wait.

items.name: reactor_run
items.signature: fn reactor_run(s: *mut Scheduler, r: *mut Reactor) -> void
items.doc:
Drives scheduler `s` and reactor `r` until both the run queue and the parked set are empty.
Suits single-threaded tests and simple drivers without the full thread-pool machinery.

items.name: fiber_sleep_ns
items.doc:
Sleeps the current fiber for `ns` nanoseconds via `IORING_OP_TIMEOUT`.
Must run inside a fiber on a thread with a reactor installed.
Returns 0 on success (kernel `-ETIME` normalised); -22 for no reactor or
negative `ns`, -12 when the SQ is full.

items.name: fiber_sleep_ms
items.doc:
Sleeps the current fiber for `ms` milliseconds.
Same return contract as `fiber_sleep_ns`; negative `ms` returns -22.

items.name: reactor_dispatch_cqe
items.signature: fn reactor_dispatch_cqe(r: *mut Reactor, cqe: *mut u8) -> void

items.name: latch_new
items.doc: Creates a heap-allocated `Latch` with the given initial count; free with `latch_free`.

items.name: latch_free
items.doc: Deallocates a `Latch` created by `latch_new`.

items.name: latch_count_down
items.doc:
Decrements the count (called by workers); wakes the waiter when the count reaches 0.

Extra calls after the count hits 0 are no-ops (no underflow).

items.name: latch_wait
items.doc:
Suspends the current fiber until the count reaches 0; returns immediately if already 0.

Single waiter only: a second concurrent `latch_wait` overwrites the first, deadlocking it.

items.name: chan_new
items.doc: Creates a heap-allocated channel holding up to `cap` items; free with `chan_free`.

items.name: chan_free
items.doc: Deallocates the channel, its ring buffer, and any pending suspended-send value.

items.name: chan_ring_write
items.signature: fn chan_ring_write(ch: *mut Chan<T>, val: T) -> void

items.name: chan_send
items.signature: fn chan_send(ch: *mut Chan<T>, val: T) -> void
items.doc:
Sends `val` into the channel; suspends the current fiber while the channel is full.

Wakes a suspended receiver if one is waiting. If another sender is already
suspended (caller error), returns immediately and `val` is silently dropped.

items.name: chan_recv
items.doc:
Receives a value from the channel; suspends the current fiber while the channel is empty.

Unblocks a suspended sender by moving its pending value into the ring. If another
receiver is already suspended (caller error), the result is undefined (null read).

items.name: spsc_push
items.signature: fn spsc_push(r: *mut SpscRing, fn_ptr: i64, arg: i64) -> bool

items.name: spsc_pop
items.signature: fn spsc_pop(r: *mut SpscRing, out_fn: *mut i64, out_arg: *mut i64) -> bool

items.name: cross_thread_enqueue
items.signature: fn cross_thread_enqueue(target_thr: i64, fiber_ptr: i64) -> void

items.name: thread_pool_new
items.doc:
Creates a pool of `n` workers, each with a scheduler, bounded SPSC inbox, eventfd,
and io_uring reactor. No OS threads run until `thread_pool_start`.

items.name: thread_pool_set_stack_pool_cap
items.signature: fn thread_pool_set_stack_pool_cap(pool: *mut ThreadPool, cap: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Configures the per-fiber stack-pool capacity on every worker's scheduler.
Must be called BEFORE `thread_pool_start` — once a worker thread is running,
its scheduler is accessed concurrently and touching it from outside is a data race.

items.name: thread_pool_start
items.doc: Spawns one OS thread per worker; each runs its reactor loop until shutdown.

items.name: thread_submit
items.signature: fn thread_submit(pool: *mut ThreadPool, target_idx: i32, f: fn(i64) -> void, arg: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Submits `f(arg)` to run as a new fiber on worker `target_idx`, waking it if asleep.
Spins while the target inbox is full. Assumes a single producer per inbox.

items.name: thread_pool_signal_done
items.doc:
Sets the shutdown flag and wakes every worker; each exits once its scheduler,
inbox, and parked I/O are all drained.

items.name: thread_pool_join
items.doc: Blocks until every worker's OS thread exits (`pthread_join` on each).

items.name: thread_pool_free
items.doc:
Frees all per-thread resources (inbox, scheduler, reactor, eventfd) and the pool itself.
Call only after `thread_pool_join`.

items.name: AF_INET
items.doc: Returns the `AF_INET` address-family constant (2).

items.name: SOCK_STREAM
items.doc: Returns the `SOCK_STREAM` socket-type constant (1).

items.name: SOCK_DGRAM
items.doc: Returns the `SOCK_DGRAM` socket-type constant (2).

items.name: sockaddr4
items.signature: fn sockaddr4(ip: u32, port: u16) -> SockAddr4
items.doc: Creates a `SockAddr4` from host-order `ip` and `port`, converting both to network byte order.

items.name: inaddr_any
items.doc: Returns `INADDR_ANY` (0.0.0.0), for binding to all interfaces.

items.name: ip4
items.doc: Builds a host-order IPv4 address from four octets, e.g. `ip4(127,0,0,1)`.

items.name: tcp_listen
items.signature: fn tcp_listen(ip: u32, port: u16, backlog: i32) -> TcpListener
items.doc:
Binds a TCP socket to `ip:port` (with `SO_REUSEADDR`) and starts listening.
Returns an invalid listener (`is_valid()` false) on error.

items.name: tcp_listen_reuseport
items.signature: fn tcp_listen_reuseport(ip: u32, port: u16, backlog: i32) -> TcpListener
items.doc:
Like `tcp_listen` but additionally sets `SO_REUSEPORT`, so multiple sockets bound to the
same `(ip, port)` can be used concurrently — the kernel load-balances `accept` across them.
Intended for per-worker listeners in multi-threaded servers (one listener per thread
eliminates the cross-thread fd handoff cost).

items.name: is_valid
items.doc: Returns `true` if the listener holds a valid (non-negative) socket fd.

items.name: accept
items.doc: Blocks until a client connects. Returns a `TcpStream` that is invalid on error.

items.name: close
items.doc: Closes the listening socket and marks the listener invalid; idempotent.

items.name: tcp_stream_from_fd
items.doc:
Builds a `TcpStream` from a raw fd (e.g. received via `serve_mt` handoff).
The caller is responsible for `fd` being a valid connected socket; the stream
assumes ownership and the fd is released via `close()`.

items.name: tcp_connect
items.signature: fn tcp_connect(ip: u32, port: u16) -> TcpStream
items.doc: Connects a blocking TCP socket to `ip:port`. Returns an invalid stream on error.

items.name: is_valid
items.doc: Returns `true` if the stream holds a valid (non-negative) socket fd.

items.name: raw_fd
items.doc: Returns the underlying fd for cross-thread handoff (`serve_mt`); ownership stays here.

items.name: close
items.doc: Closes the socket and marks the stream invalid; idempotent.

items.name: write_vectored
items.signature: fn write_vectored(self: &mut TcpStream, iovs: *const IoSlice, nvecs: i32) -> i64
items.doc:
Performs a vectored synchronous write (`writev`). Returns total bytes written (>= 0)
or negative on error. `IoSlice` layout matches `struct iovec`.

items.name: read
items.signature: fn read(self: &mut TcpStream, buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: write
items.signature: fn write(self: &mut TcpStream, buf: *const u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: udp_bind
items.signature: fn udp_bind(ip: u32, port: u16) -> UdpSocket
items.doc: Creates a UDP socket bound to `ip:port`. Returns an invalid socket (`is_valid()` false) on error.

items.name: is_valid
items.doc: Returns `true` if the socket holds a valid (non-negative) fd.

items.name: recv_from
items.signature: fn recv_from(self: &UdpSocket, buf: *mut u8, len: i64, from: &mut SockAddr4) -> i64
items.doc:
Blocks until a datagram arrives; fills `buf` and stores the sender address in `from`.
Returns bytes received (>= 0) or negative on error.

items.name: send_to
items.signature: fn send_to(self: &UdpSocket, buf: *const u8, len: i64, to: &SockAddr4) -> i64
items.doc:
Sends `len` bytes from `buf` as one datagram to `to`.
Returns bytes sent (>= 0) or negative on error.

items.name: close
items.doc: Closes the socket and marks it invalid; idempotent.

items.name: async_accept
items.doc:
Awaits an incoming connection via io_uring, suspending the calling fiber.
Returns an invalid `TcpStream` on error (negative errno in the fd; -1 if no
current `Reactor` or the SQ ring is full).

items.name: async_recv
items.signature: fn async_recv(self: &TcpStream, buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Awaits up to `len` bytes from the socket; returns bytes read (>= 0) or negative errno.
Suspends the current fiber until the CQE arrives. `len` is clamped to `i32` max.

items.name: async_write_vectored
items.signature: fn async_write_vectored(self: &TcpStream, iovs: *const IoSlice, nvecs: i32) -> i64
items.doc:
Awaits a vectored write; returns total bytes written (>= 0) or negative errno.
The `iovs` array must remain valid for the lifetime of the parked fiber —
the caller's stack is the natural holder.

items.name: async_send
items.signature: fn async_send(self: &TcpStream, buf: *const u8, len: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Awaits send of `len` bytes, suspending the calling fiber; returns bytes written
(>= 0) or negative errno. `len` is clamped to `i32` max.

items.name: async_tcp_stream
items.doc: Wraps `s` in an `AsyncTcpStream`, taking ownership.

items.name: is_valid
items.doc: Returns `true` if the underlying stream is valid.

items.name: close
items.doc: Closes the underlying stream; idempotent.

items.name: read
items.signature: fn read(self: &mut AsyncTcpStream, buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: write
items.signature: fn write(self: &mut AsyncTcpStream, buf: *const u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: async_tcp_connect
items.signature: fn async_tcp_connect(ip: u32, port: u16) -> TcpStream
items.doc:
Connects a fresh TCP socket to `ip:port` via io_uring, suspending the calling fiber.
Returns an invalid `TcpStream` on error (negative errno in the fd; -1 if no
current `Reactor`).

items.name: http_send
items.signature: fn http_send(host_ip: u32, port: u16, req: &Request) -> Result
items.doc:
Sends `req` to `host_ip:port` over a fresh TCP connection and reads the response.
Reads until the peer closes or a complete message is buffered.
`host_ip` must be numeric IPv4 — DNS resolution is not implemented.

items.name: http_get_at
items.signature: fn http_get_at(host_ip: u32, port: u16, host_hdr: &[u8], path: &[u8]) -> Result
items.doc:
Issues a GET for `path` against `host_ip:port`, building a minimal `Request`
with a `Host` header set to `host_hdr`.

items.name: http_get
items.doc:
Parses `url_str` as an absolute HTTP URL (`http://host[:port]/path[?query]`)
and issues a GET.  Only numeric IPv4 hosts are supported; a hostname or a
non-`http` scheme yields `HttpError::BadUrl`.

items.name: validate_request_target
items.signature: fn validate_request_target(p: *const u8, n: i64) -> bool

items.name: validate_token
items.signature: fn validate_token(p: *const u8, n: i64) -> bool

items.name: validate_header_value
items.signature: fn validate_header_value(p: *const u8, n: i64) -> bool

items.name: find_crlf
items.signature: fn find_crlf(src: *const u8, len: i64, from: i64) -> i64
items.doc: Returns the index of the first CRLF's CR in `[from, len)`, or -1 if absent.

items.name: parse_decimal
items.signature: fn parse_decimal(src: *const u8, from: i64, end: i64) -> i64

items.name: trim_ows_left
items.signature: fn trim_ows_left(src: *const u8, from: i64, end: i64) -> i64

items.name: trim_ows_right
items.signature: fn trim_ows_right(src: *const u8, from: i64, end: i64) -> i64

items.name: copy_body
items.signature: fn copy_body(src: *const u8, from: i64, end: i64, dst: &mut Vec<u8>) -> void

items.name: parse_headers
items.signature: fn parse_headers(src: *const u8, len: i64, pos: i64, h: &mut Headers) -> i64
items.doc:
Parses header lines from `[pos, len)` into `h`, appending each field.
Returns the position just past the empty-CRLF terminator, or -1 on malformed
input.  Names must be RFC 7230 tokens; values are OWS-trimmed.

items.name: body_length
items.doc:
Returns the body-framing decision for headers `h`.
`>= 0`: Content-Length bytes; `-1`: no framing (caller decides; response →
read to EOF, request → empty); `-2`: chunked; `-3`: malformed (invalid or
conflicting Content-Length, non-`chunked` Transfer-Encoding, or TE with CL).

items.name: parse_request_buf
items.signature: fn parse_request_buf(src: *const u8, len: i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Parses a complete HTTP request (request-line, headers, body) from `[src, src+len)`.
Body framing follows `body_length`: Content-Length is copied, chunked is decoded,
no framing yields an empty body.  Partial input is `HttpError::Parse` — the buffer
must hold the whole message.

items.name: parse_response_buf
items.signature: fn parse_response_buf(src: *const u8, len: i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Parses a complete HTTP response (status-line, headers, body) from `[src, src+len)`.
Body framing follows `body_length`: with no framing, the remainder of the buffer
becomes the body (read-to-EOF semantics).

items.name: resolve_request_target
items.signature: fn resolve_request_target(tp: *const u8, tn: i64) -> Url

items.name: parse_header_line
items.signature: fn parse_header_line(hp: *const u8, hn: i64, out: &mut Headers) -> bool

items.name: parse_request_stream
items.doc:
Reads and parses a request head (request-line + headers) from `br`; no body is read.
Leaves `br` at the first body byte; the returned `Request.body` is empty — frame it
via `body_length(&req.headers)`.  Stricter than `parse_request_buf`: requires
HTTP/1.0|1.1, origin-/asterisk-form target, Host on 1.1, and no Transfer-Encoding on 1.0.

items.name: parse_response_stream
items.doc:
Reads and parses a response head (status-line + headers) from `br`; no body is read.
Leaves `br` at the first body byte; the returned `Response.body` is empty — frame it
via `body_length(&resp.headers)`.

items.name: write_i64_decimal
items.signature: fn write_i64_decimal(v: i64, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> void
items.doc:
Appends the decimal ASCII representation of `v` to `out`.
Supports non-negative values only; `v < 0` appends nothing.

items.name: serialize_request
items.doc:
Appends the full HTTP/1.1 request — request-line, headers, CRLF, body — to `out`.
Wire form: `METHOD /path?query HTTP/1.1\r\n<headers>\r\n<body>`; empty path serializes as `/`,
`?query` only when non-empty. Headers are written as stored; no `Content-Length` is synthesized.

items.name: serialize_response
items.doc:
Appends the full HTTP/1.1 response — status-line, headers, CRLF, body — to `out`.
Wire form: `HTTP/1.1 <status> <reason>\r\n<headers>\r\n<body>`; headers are written as stored.

items.name: serialize_request_stream
items.doc:
Serializes the full request into one buffer and writes it to `w` via a single `write_all`.
Returns `0` on success, negative `errno` on error.

items.name: serialize_response_stream
items.doc:
Serializes the full response into one buffer and writes it to `w` via a single `write_all`.
Returns `0` on success, negative `errno` on error.

items.name: serialize_response_head_stream
items.doc:
Writes only the response head — status-line, headers, terminator CRLF — to `w`.
Use when the body is large or framed by a different encoder (`ChunkedWriter`, an open
file, ...); the caller writes the body afterwards through the same writer.
Returns `0` on success, negative `errno` on error.

items.name: serialize_request_head_stream
items.doc:
Writes only the request head — request-line, headers, terminator CRLF — to `w`.
Request counterpart of `serialize_response_head_stream`; the caller writes the body afterwards.
Returns `0` on success, negative `errno` on error.

items.name: read_request_message
items.doc:
Drains a full HTTP/1.1 request message from `s` into `buf`.
Reads until headers are complete AND the entire Content-Length body (or chunked
terminator) has arrived, or the peer closes. Returns `Ok(bytes_in_buf)` or `Err`
on IO / framing failure.

items.name: send_error_response
items.signature: fn send_error_response(sink: &mut AsyncTcpStream, status: i32) -> i64

items.name: serve_connection
items.signature: fn serve_connection(stream: TcpStream, svc: &mut S) -> i64
items.doc:
Serves HTTP/1.1 requests from `stream` through `svc` in a keep-alive loop.
Handles `Expect: 100-continue`; drains `Content-Length` bodies and decodes chunked
bodies into `req.body`. Closes on `Connection: close` from either side or on a
framing/IO error (malformed requests get a 400 reply first). Always returns 0.

items.name: serve_bounded
items.signature: fn serve_bounded(port: u16, count: i64, svc: &mut S) -> Result
items.doc:
Accepts up to `count` connections serially, running each through `serve_connection`
(keep-alive works per connection). Returns `Ok(())` after `count` connections have
been served or accept fails; `Err(Connect)` if bind fails. For tests and
"single request batch" scenarios.

items.name: serve_until
items.signature: fn serve_until(port: u16, svc: &mut S, stop_flag: *mut i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Accepts connections until `*stop_flag != 0`, serving each through `serve_connection`.
The stop flag must be an `i64` cell the caller mutates from another fiber / signal
handler — checked between accepts; accept failure also stops. Returns `Ok(())` on
clean stop or `Err(Connect)` if bind fails.

items.name: serve_n
items.signature: fn serve_n(port: u16, count: i64, handler: fn(&Request) -> Response) -> Result
items.doc:
Binds `port`, accepts `count` connections serially, invokes `handler` for each,
writes the serialized response back, and closes — one request per connection.
Returns `Ok(())` once `count` connections have been served, or `Err(Connect)` on
fatal bind/accept failure.

items.name: serve_mt
items.signature: fn serve_mt(port: u16, n_workers: i32, svc: &mut FnService, stop_flag: *mut i64) -> Result
items.doc:
Runs a multi-worker server: accepts on the calling thread's reactor and hands each
connection round-robin to one of `n_workers` worker threads via `serve_connection`.
Stops when `*stop_flag != 0` or accept fails; `Err(Connect)` if bind fails or
`n_workers < 1`. Safety: `svc` is shared by raw pointer across workers — safe only
for stateless services (e.g. `FnService`); stateful services need their own sync.

items.name: serve_mt_bounded
items.signature: fn serve_mt_bounded(port: u16, n_workers: i32, count: i64, svc: &mut FnService) -> *mut ThreadPool
items.doc:
Accepts exactly `count` connections, handing each round-robin to `n_workers`
workers, then signals shutdown and returns the pool without joining it.
Caller must `thread_pool_join` + `thread_pool_free` only after its own reactor has
drained — joining inside the fiber would block the OS thread and deadlock peers.

items.name: fn_service
items.doc: Wraps `f` in a `FnService` so a fn-pointer handler can be used as a `Service`.

items.name: vec_push_u8
items.signature: fn vec_push_u8(v: &mut Vec<u8>, b: u8) -> void
items.doc: Pushes byte `b` onto `v`.

items.name: vec_len_u8
items.doc: Returns the number of bytes in `v`.

items.name: vec_get_u8
items.doc: Returns the byte at index `i` of `v`.

items.name: vec_at_const_u8
items.doc: Returns a raw const pointer to the byte at index `i` of `v`.

items.name: s2str
items.doc: Borrows a `String`'s contents as a `str` (no copy).

items.name: bytes_eq
items.signature: fn bytes_eq(a: *const u8, an: i64, b: *const u8, bn: i64) -> bool
items.doc: Compares two byte ranges exactly (ASCII, case-sensitive).

items.name: ascii_ieq
items.signature: fn ascii_ieq(a: *const u8, an: i64, b: *const u8, bn: i64) -> bool
items.doc: Compares two byte ranges case-insensitively (ASCII only).

items.name: get
items.doc: Returns the `GET` method.

items.name: post
items.doc: Returns the `POST` method.

items.name: put
items.doc: Returns the `PUT` method.

items.name: delete
items.doc: Returns the `DELETE` method.

items.name: head
items.doc: Returns the `HEAD` method.

items.name: options
items.doc: Returns the `OPTIONS` method.

items.name: patch
items.doc: Returns the `PATCH` method.

items.name: eq_str
items.doc: Returns `true` if the method token equals `s` exactly (case-sensitive).

items.name: method_parse
items.doc:
Parses a method token (ASCII, case-sensitive per RFC 7230).
Unknown tokens return `code == 7` with the original name preserved.

items.name: new
items.doc: Creates an empty header list.

items.name: len
items.doc: Returns the number of header entries.

items.name: append
items.signature: fn append(self: &mut Headers, name: &[u8], value: &[u8]) -> void
items.doc: Appends a `(name, value)` pair unconditionally (preserves duplicates).

items.name: contains
items.doc: Returns `true` if any header matches `name` (ASCII case-insensitive).

items.name: get
items.doc: Returns the value of the first header matching `name`, or `None`.

items.name: set
items.signature: fn set(self: &mut Headers, name: &[u8], value: &[u8]) -> void
items.doc:
Replaces the first header matching `name` with `value`, or appends if none matches.
Later duplicates of `name` are left in place.

items.name: remove
items.doc: Removes all headers matching `name`; returns the number removed.

items.name: new
items.signature: fn new(method: Method, u: Url) -> Request
items.doc: Creates a request with empty headers and body; `version_minor` defaults to 1.

items.name: new
items.doc: Creates a response with the canonical reason phrase for `status`, empty headers and body.

items.name: http_reason_phrase
items.doc: Returns the standard reason phrase for `status`; unknown codes yield `""`.

items.name: write_hex_len
items.signature: fn write_hex_len(n: i64, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> void

items.name: chunked_write_chunk
items.signature: fn chunked_write_chunk(data: *const u8, n: i64, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> void
items.doc:
Appends one chunk (hex-length + CRLF + data + CRLF) to `out`.
`n == 0` emits bytes identical to the stream terminator; use `chunked_finalize` for that.

items.name: chunked_finalize
items.doc: Appends the chunked-stream terminator (`0\r\n\r\n`) to `out`.

items.name: chunked_encode
items.signature: fn chunked_encode(data: *const u8, n: i64, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> void
items.doc:
Encodes a whole body as one chunk plus the zero-length terminator, appended to `out`.
For streaming, call `chunked_write_chunk` repeatedly, then `chunked_finalize`.

items.name: chunked_decode
items.signature: fn chunked_decode(src: *const u8, len: i64, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> i64
items.doc:
Decodes chunked bytes `[src, src+len)`, appending body data to `out`.
Returns total input bytes consumed (through the final trailer CRLF), or -1 on malformed input.
Trailer headers are parsed and skipped.

items.name: chunked_reader_wrap
items.doc:
Wraps `r` in a `ChunkedReader`, taking ownership.
To keep the underlying reader available afterwards, use `read_chunked_body` instead.

items.name: read_chunked_body
items.signature: fn read_chunked_body(r: &mut R, out: &mut Vec<u8>) -> i64
items.doc:
Reads a full chunked-encoded body from `r`, appending decoded bytes to `out`.
Returns bytes appended on success, -1 on malformed input / I/O error.
Borrows `r` (unlike `ChunkedReader`), so e.g. an HTTP server can read the next request
from the same `BufReader` afterwards.

items.name: content_length_reader_wrap
items.signature: fn content_length_reader_wrap(r: R, n: i64) -> ContentLengthReader<R>
items.doc: Wraps `r` in a `ContentLengthReader` capped at `n` bytes; negative `n` is clamped to 0.

items.name: chunked_writer_wrap
items.doc: Wraps `w` in a `ChunkedWriter`, taking ownership of the sink.

items.name: cw_write_hex_len
items.signature: fn cw_write_hex_len(w: &mut W, n: i64) -> i64

items.name: tls_slots
items.doc: Returns the number of per-thread TLS slots available (`0..tls_slots()`).

items.name: tls_get
items.doc: Reads TLS slot `key`.  Returns 0 if never set on this thread.

items.name: tls_set
items.signature: fn tls_set(key: i64, val: i64) -> void
items.doc: Writes `val` into TLS slot `key`.

items.name: pipe_new
items.doc: Creates a pipe via `pipe2(2)`. Returns a `Pipe` with both ends open, or `Pipe{-1,-1}` on error.

items.name: pipe_new_nonblock
items.doc: Creates a pipe with both ends in `O_NONBLOCK` mode. Returns `Pipe{-1,-1}` on error.

items.name: is_valid
items.doc: Returns `true` if both pipe ends hold open (non-negative) descriptors.

items.name: close_read
items.doc: Closes the read end and sets `read_fd` to `-1`; no-op if already closed.

items.name: close_write
items.doc: Closes the write end and sets `write_fd` to `-1`; no-op if already closed.

items.name: close
items.doc: Closes both ends of the pipe; idempotent.

items.name: write_vectored
items.signature: fn write_vectored(self: &mut Pipe, iovs: *const IoSlice, nvecs: i32) -> i64
items.doc:
Performs a vectored write (`writev`) to the pipe's write end.
Returns total bytes written (>= 0) or negative on error.
`IoSlice` layout matches `struct iovec`.

items.name: read_vectored
items.signature: fn read_vectored(self: &mut Pipe, iovs: *mut IoSlice, nvecs: i32) -> i64
items.doc:
Performs a vectored read (`readv`) from the pipe's read end.
Returns bytes read (0 = EOF) or negative on error.

items.name: read
items.signature: fn read(self: &mut Pipe, buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: write
items.signature: fn write(self: &mut Pipe, buf: *const u8, len: i64) -> i64

items.name: compile
items.doc: Compiles pattern `p` into a `Regex` that borrows `p`'s bytes; `p` must outlive it.

items.name: atom_length
items.signature: fn atom_length(pat: *const u8, plen: i64, pi: i64) -> i64

items.name: escape_matches
items.signature: fn escape_matches(c: u8, b: u8) -> bool

items.name: atom_matches
items.signature: fn atom_matches(pat: *const u8, pi: i64, alen: i64, b: u8) -> bool

items.name: match_here
items.signature: fn match_here(pat: *const u8, plen: i64, pi: i64, text: *const u8, tlen: i64, ti: i64) -> bool

items.name: match_star
items.signature: fn match_star(pat: *const u8, plen: i64, atom_pi: i64, alen: i64, text: *const u8, tlen: i64, ti0: i64) -> bool

items.name: is_match
items.doc: Returns `true` if `re` matches anywhere in `s`; `^` anchors to start, `$` to end.

items.name: find
items.doc: Returns the byte index of the first match in `s`, or `-1` if none.

items.name: TRACE
items.doc: Returns the `TRACE` level code (0), the most verbose level.

items.name: DEBUG
items.doc: Returns the `DEBUG` level code (1).

items.name: INFO
items.doc: Returns the `INFO` level code (2), the default threshold.

items.name: WARN
items.doc: Returns the `WARN` level code (3).

items.name: ERROR
items.doc: Returns the `ERROR` level code (4).

items.name: OFF
items.doc: Returns the `OFF` level code (100); as a threshold it suppresses all messages.

items.name: write_all
items.signature: fn write_all(buf: *const u8, n: i64) -> void

items.name: threshold
items.doc:
Returns the current minimum log level, read from the `LOGOS_LOG` env variable.
Recognizes TRACE/DEBUG/INFO/WARN/ERROR/OFF (case-insensitive); unset or
unrecognized values yield `INFO()`. Re-reads the environment on every call.

items.name: level_prefix
items.signature: fn level_prefix(level: i32, out: &mut String) -> void

items.name: emit
log_kv_i64
items.signature: fn log_kv_i64(level: i32, msg: &[u8], key: &[u8], value: i64) -> void
items.doc:
Writes `msg` followed by a ` key=value` pair (integer) to stderr as one line.
Silently dropped when `level` is below `threshold()`.

items.name: log_kv_str
items.signature: fn log_kv_str(level: i32, msg: &[u8], key: &[u8], value: &[u8]) -> void
items.doc:
Writes `msg` followed by a ` key="value"` pair to stderr as one line.
Silently dropped when `level` is below `threshold()`. `value` is quoted but
NOT escaped — embedded `"` or newlines pass through verbatim.

items.name: trace
items.doc: Logs `msg` at the `TRACE` level.

items.name: debug
items.doc: Logs `msg` at the `DEBUG` level.

items.name: info
items.doc: Logs `msg` at the `INFO` level.

items.name: warn
items.doc: Logs `msg` at the `WARN` level.

items.name: error
items.doc: Logs `msg` at the `ERROR` level.

items.name: pid
items.doc: Returns the process id of the calling process.

items.name: ppid
items.doc: Returns the process id of the parent of the calling process.

items.name: uid
items.doc: Returns the real user id of the calling process.

items.name: euid
items.doc: Returns the effective user id of the calling process.

items.name: gid
items.doc: Returns the real group id of the calling process.

items.name: hostname
items.doc:
Returns the system hostname.

Returns an empty `String` if `gethostname` fails.

items.name: arch
items.doc:
Returns the CPU architecture the program was compiled for.

Hard-coded to `"x86_64"` until cross-compilation lands.

items.name: os
items.doc:
Returns the operating system the program was compiled for.

Hard-coded to `"linux"` until cross-compilation lands.

items.name: family
items.doc:
Returns the OS family the program was compiled for.

Hard-coded to `"unix"` until cross-compilation lands.

items.name: exit_process
items.doc:
Terminates the current process with `code` as the exit status.

Never returns; live locals are not dropped (calls libc `exit`).

items.name: SIGHUP
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGHUP` signal number (terminal hangup).

items.name: SIGINT
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGINT` signal number (keyboard interrupt, Ctrl-C).

items.name: SIGQUIT
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGQUIT` signal number (keyboard quit, Ctrl-\).

items.name: SIGILL
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGILL` signal number (illegal instruction).

items.name: SIGABRT
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGABRT` signal number (abort).

items.name: SIGFPE
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGFPE` signal number (erroneous arithmetic operation).

items.name: SIGKILL
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGKILL` signal number (kill); cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored.

items.name: SIGSEGV
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGSEGV` signal number (invalid memory reference).

items.name: SIGPIPE
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGPIPE` signal number (write to a pipe with no readers).

items.name: SIGALRM
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGALRM` signal number (timer set by `schedule_alarm` expired).

items.name: SIGTERM
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGTERM` signal number (termination request).

items.name: SIGUSR1
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGUSR1` signal number (user-defined signal 1).

items.name: SIGUSR2
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGUSR2` signal number (user-defined signal 2).

items.name: SIGCHLD
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGCHLD` signal number (child stopped or terminated).

items.name: SIGCONT
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGCONT` signal number (continue if stopped).

items.name: SIGSTOP
items.doc: Returns the Linux `SIGSTOP` signal number (stop process); cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored.

items.name: SIG_BLOCK
items.doc: Returns the `sigprocmask` `how` value that adds the given set to the blocked mask.

items.name: SIG_UNBLOCK
items.doc: Returns the `sigprocmask` `how` value that removes the given set from the blocked mask.

items.name: SIG_SETMASK
items.doc: Returns the `sigprocmask` `how` value that replaces the blocked mask with the given set.

items.name: SIG_DFL
items.doc: Returns the default-action handler sentinel; cast via `as *const u8` for `install`.

items.name: SIG_IGN
items.doc: Returns the ignore-signal handler sentinel; cast via `as *const u8` for `install`.

items.name: install
items.signature: fn install(signum: i32, handler: *const u8) -> *const u8
items.doc:
Installs a signal handler for `signum` and returns the previous handler pointer.
`handler` must be a `fn(i32)` function pointer (cast via `as *const u8`), or the
`SIG_DFL()`/`SIG_IGN()` sentinels. Handlers run asynchronously: async-signal-safe code only.

items.name: reset_default
items.doc: Restores the default action (`SIG_DFL`) for `signum`.

items.name: ignore
items.doc: Sets the disposition of `signum` to ignore (`SIG_IGN`).

items.name: send_signal
items.signature: fn send_signal(pid: i32, signum: i32) -> i32
items.doc: Sends `signum` to process `pid` via kill(2); returns 0 on success, -1 on error.

items.name: raise_self
items.doc: Sends `signum` to the calling thread via raise(2); returns 0 on success, nonzero on error.

items.name: block
items.doc: Blocks delivery of `signum` in the current thread's signal mask; returns 0 on success.

items.name: unblock
items.doc: Unblocks delivery of `signum` in the current thread's signal mask; returns 0 on success.

items.name: schedule_alarm
items.doc:
Schedules a `SIGALRM` after `seconds` seconds; `seconds == 0` cancels any pending alarm.
Returns seconds remaining on a previously scheduled alarm, or 0 if none.

items.name: manually_drop_new
items.doc: Wraps `v` in `ManuallyDrop`, suppressing its destructor at scope-exit.

items.name: manually_drop_into_inner
items.doc:
Moves the inner value out of the wrapper, consuming it.
The returned value behaves normally: its `Drop`, if any, fires at the caller's scope-exit.

items.name: manually_drop_drop
items.doc:
Runs the inner value's `Drop` in place, without consuming the wrapper.
Mirrors Rust's `unsafe fn ManuallyDrop::drop` (`ptr::drop_in_place` on the inner value).
Unsafe: after this the inner value is logically gone — do not read it or drop it again.

items.name: maybe_dangling_new
items.doc: Wraps `v` in `MaybeDangling`.

items.name: maybe_dangling_into_inner
items.doc: Moves the inner value out, consuming the wrapper.

items.name: alignment_new
items.doc: Returns `Some(Alignment)` if `n` is a power of 2 > 0, else `None`.

items.name: alignment_new_unchecked
items.doc:
Creates an `Alignment` without the power-of-2 check (Rust's `Alignment::new_unchecked`).
Unsafe: caller must guarantee `n` is a nonzero power of 2.

items.name: as_u64
items.doc: Returns the alignment in bytes.

items.name: log2
items.doc: Returns log2 of the alignment (trailing-zero count of `bytes`).

items.name: drop_guard_new
items.doc: Creates an armed `DropGuard` that runs `f` on drop.

items.name: drop_guard_disarm_into
items.doc:
Consumes the guard, returning the closure `f` without running it.
Extracting `f` by value suppresses the guard's `Drop`.

items.name: box_pin
items.doc:
Heap-pins `val`, moving it into a new `Box` first.
Sound for any `T`: the value's unpinned phase ends at this call, before pinning.

items.name: box_new
items.doc: Allocates a heap block sized for `T`, moves `val` into it, and returns the `Box`.

items.name: box_into_raw
items.doc:
Consumes the `Box` and returns its raw heap pointer without running `Box`'s `Drop`.
The heap block is NOT freed — ownership of the allocation transfers to the caller,
who must reconstitute a `Box` via `box_from_raw` or free it manually.

items.name: box_take
items.doc:
Consumes the `Box`, moves its contents out (no drop of the contents — ownership
transfers to the caller), and frees the heap block.
The compiler desugars `let x = *b` over a move-typed `Box` to this call.

items.name: box_from_raw
items.doc:
Reconstitutes a `Box` from a raw pointer previously produced by `box_into_raw`
(or any pointer to a heap block allocated with the matching layout).
The `Box` re-takes ownership: its `Drop` will run `T`'s destructor and free the block.
Unsafe — caller guarantees `raw` is a live, uniquely-owned allocation of `T`.

items.name: box_leak
items.doc:
Consumes the `Box` and leaks its contents, returning a `&mut T` that lives for
the rest of the program. The allocation is never freed.

items.name: success
items.doc: Returns `true` if the process exited normally with exit code 0.

items.name: exited
items.doc: Returns `true` if the process exited normally (was not killed by a signal).

items.name: code
items.doc: Returns the exit code when `exited()` is true; 0 otherwise.

items.name: signal
items.doc: Returns the terminating signal number if killed by a signal; 0 otherwise.

items.name: pid
items.doc: Returns the child's OS process id (-1 if `spawn` failed to fork).

items.name: write_stdin
items.signature: fn write_stdin(self: &Child, buf: *const u8, len: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Writes up to `len` bytes from `buf` to the child's stdin pipe.
Returns bytes written, or -1 if stdin was not piped at spawn time.

items.name: read_stdout
items.signature: fn read_stdout(self: &Child, buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Reads up to `len` bytes from the child's stdout pipe into `buf`.
Returns bytes read (0 at EOF), or -1 if stdout was not piped at spawn time.

items.name: close_stdin
items.doc: Closes the child's stdin pipe, signaling EOF to the child. No-op if already closed.

items.name: close_stdout
items.doc: Closes the child's stdout pipe. No-op if already closed.

items.name: close_stderr
items.doc: Closes the child's stderr pipe. No-op if already closed.

items.name: wait
items.doc:
Blocks until the child exits and returns its `ExitStatus`.
Closes any still-open stdin/stdout/stderr pipes before returning.

items.name: spawn
items.signature: fn spawn(argv: *const *const u8, pipe_stdin: bool, pipe_stdout: bool, pipe_stderr: bool) -> Child
items.doc:
Spawns a child process via `fork` + `execvp`.
`argv` must be a null-terminated array of NUL-terminated C strings; `argv[0]` is the program.
Each `pipe_*` flag redirects that child fd through a pipe held open on the returned `Child`.
On fork failure returns a `Child` with pid -1; if exec fails the child exits with code 127.

items.name: spawn_simple
items.doc: Spawns a child with no pipes; the child inherits the parent's stdio fds.

items.name: filter
items.signature: fn filter(self: LineReader, pred: fn(String) -> bool) -> FilterIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: map
items.signature: fn map(self: LineReader, f: MapFn) -> MapIter<LineReader, String, MapOut, MapFn>

items.name: take
items.signature: fn take(self: LineReader, n: i64) -> TakeIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: skip
items.signature: fn skip(self: LineReader, n: i64) -> SkipIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: chain
items.signature: fn chain(self: LineReader, other: ChainOther) -> ChainIter<LineReader, ChainOther, String>

items.name: zip
items.signature: fn zip(self: LineReader, other: ZipOther) -> ZipIter<LineReader, ZipOther, String, ZipOtherItem>

items.name: min_by
items.signature: fn min_by(self: LineReader, cmp: fn(String, String) -> i32) -> Option

items.name: max_by
items.signature: fn max_by(self: LineReader, cmp: fn(String, String) -> i32) -> Option

items.name: take_while
items.signature: fn take_while(self: LineReader, pred: fn(String) -> bool, zero: String) -> TakeWhileIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: skip_while
items.signature: fn skip_while(self: LineReader, pred: fn(String) -> bool, zero: String) -> SkipWhileIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: step_by
items.signature: fn step_by(self: LineReader, step: i64, zero: String) -> StepByIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: inspect
items.signature: fn inspect(self: LineReader, visit: fn(String) -> void, zero: String) -> InspectIter<LineReader, String>

items.name: fold
items.signature: fn fold(self: LineReader, init: Acc, f: FoldFn) -> Acc

items.name: try_fold
items.signature: fn try_fold(self: LineReader, init: Acc, f: TryFoldFn) -> ControlFlow

items.name: find_map
items.signature: fn find_map(self: LineReader, f: FindMapFn) -> Option

items.name: reduce
items.signature: fn reduce(self: LineReader, f: ReduceFn) -> Option

items.name: for_each
items.signature: fn for_each(self: LineReader, f: EachFn) -> void

items.name: try_for_each
items.signature: fn try_for_each(self: LineReader, f: TryEachFn) -> ControlFlow

items.name: any
items.signature: fn any(self: LineReader, pred: AnyFn) -> bool

items.name: all
items.signature: fn all(self: LineReader, pred: AllFn) -> bool

items.name: nth
items.signature: fn nth(self: &mut LineReader, n: i64) -> Option

items.name: find
items.signature: fn find(self: &mut LineReader, pred: FindFn) -> Option

items.name: position
items.signature: fn position(self: &mut LineReader, pred: PosFn) -> Option

items.name: stdout_lines
items.doc:
Returns a `LineReader` iterating lines from the child's stdout.
Requires stdout to have been piped at spawn time (`pipe_stdout = true`).

items.name: stderr_lines
items.doc: Returns a `LineReader` over the child's stderr lines; same semantics as `stdout_lines`.

items.name: os_random
items.signature: fn os_random(buf: *mut u8, len: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Fills `buf` with `len` cryptographic-quality random bytes from the OS.
Returns the number of bytes written (`len` on success) or -1 on error.

items.name: os_u64
items.doc:
Returns a random `u64` from OS entropy.
The `getrandom` result is unchecked; if it fails, the zero-initialized buffer yields 0.

items.name: rng_new
items.doc: Creates an `Rng` with the given seed; equal seeds yield identical sequences.

items.name: rng_from_os
items.doc: Creates an `Rng` seeded from OS entropy.

items.name: next_u64
items.doc: Returns the next pseudo-random `u64` (one raw splitmix64 step).

items.name: next_u32
items.doc: Returns the next pseudo-random `u32` (low 32 bits of `next_u64`).

items.name: next_range_i64
items.signature: fn next_range_i64(self: &mut Rng, lo: i64, hi: i64) -> i64
items.doc:
Returns a pseudo-random `i64` uniform in `[lo, hi)`.
Caller must ensure `hi > lo` (`hi == lo` divides by zero); modulo reduction gives
negligible bias unless the span approaches 2^64.

items.name: next_f64
items.doc: Returns a pseudo-random `f64` uniform in `[0.0, 1.0)` with 53 bits of precision.

items.name: next_bool
items.doc: Returns a pseudo-random `bool`, each value with probability 1/2.

items.name: emit_bytes
items.signature: fn emit_bytes(p: *const u8, n: i64) -> void

items.name: new
items.doc: Creates a `TestCtx` with zero counts and no current test name.

items.name: passed
items.doc: Returns the number of passed assertions.

items.name: failed
items.doc: Returns the number of failed assertions.

items.name: begin
items.doc: Starts a named test case; `name` is included in failure messages until the next `begin`.

items.name: record_fail
items.signature: fn record_fail(self: &mut TestCtx, kind: &[u8], detail: &[u8]) -> void

items.name: assert_true
items.signature: fn assert_true(self: &mut TestCtx, cond: bool) -> void
items.doc: Asserts `cond` is true; a failure is recorded and execution continues (no abort).

items.name: assert_false
items.signature: fn assert_false(self: &mut TestCtx, cond: bool) -> void
items.doc: Asserts `cond` is false; a failure is recorded and execution continues (no abort).

items.name: assert_eq_i64
items.signature: fn assert_eq_i64(self: &mut TestCtx, actual: i64, expected: i64) -> void
items.doc: Asserts `actual == expected`; the failure diagnostic includes both values.

items.name: assert_eq_i32
items.signature: fn assert_eq_i32(self: &mut TestCtx, actual: i32, expected: i32) -> void
items.doc: Asserts `actual == expected`, widening to `i64` and delegating to `assert_eq_i64`.

items.name: assert_eq_str
items.signature: fn assert_eq_str(self: &mut TestCtx, actual: &[u8], expected: &[u8]) -> void
items.doc: Asserts `actual` and `expected` are byte-for-byte equal.

items.name: fail
items.doc: Records an unconditional failure with `reason` in the diagnostic.

items.name: summary
items.doc: Prints a pass/fail summary to stderr; returns exit code 0 on success, 1 on any failure.

items.name: id
items.doc: Returns the underlying pthread thread ID.

items.name: join
items.doc:
Waits for the thread to finish and returns the raw exit pointer from the
thread function (whatever it returned, cast to `*mut u8`).
Returns null if the handle was already joined or detached.

items.name: detach
items.doc:
Detaches the thread: it runs to completion independently and can no longer be joined.
No-op if the handle was already joined or detached.

items.name: thread_spawn
items.signature: fn thread_spawn(start: *const u8, arg: *mut u8) -> JoinHandle
items.doc:
Spawns a new OS thread running `start(arg)`.
`start` must be a function pointer with signature `fn(*mut u8) -> *mut u8`
(cast from a concrete Logos fn using `as *const u8`); `arg` is passed through unchanged.
On failure returns a `JoinHandle` with `tid == -1`, already marked joined.

items.name: current_id
items.doc: Returns the calling thread's pthread ID (opaque; comparable with `==`).

items.name: yield_now
items.doc: Hints to the scheduler that the current thread is willing to yield to others.

items.name: days_from_civil
items.signature: fn days_from_civil(y: i64, m: i32, d: i32) -> i64

items.name: pmod
items.signature: fn pmod(a: i64, b: i64) -> i64

items.name: fdiv
items.signature: fn fdiv(a: i64, b: i64) -> i64

items.name: from_unix_secs
items.doc: Breaks a Unix-epoch second count into UTC civil time, including `wday`.

items.name: to_unix_secs
items.doc:
Converts a `CivilTime` (UTC) back to Unix seconds.
`wday` is ignored; fields are not range-checked.

items.name: is_leap
items.doc: Returns `true` if year `y` is a leap year in the proleptic Gregorian calendar.

items.name: days_in_month
items.signature: fn days_in_month(y: i64, m: i32) -> i32
items.doc: Returns the number of days in month `m` of year `y`, or `0` if `m` is outside `1..=12`.

items.name: push_u_pad
items.signature: fn push_u_pad(out: &mut String, v: i64, width: i32) -> void

items.name: format_iso8601
items.doc:
Appends `ct` to `out` as ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ` (UTC).
Negative years are written with a leading `-`; the year is zero-padded to 4 digits.

items.name: format_date
items.doc: Appends the date of `ct` to `out` as `YYYY-MM-DD` (leading `-` for negative years).

items.name: format_time
items.doc: Appends the time of day of `ct` to `out` as `HH:MM:SS`.

items.name: weekday_name
items.doc: Returns the full English weekday name for `w` (`0` = `"Sunday"` … `6` = `"Saturday"`), or `""`.

items.name: month_name
items.doc: Returns the full English month name for `m` in `1..=12`, or `""` otherwise.

items.name: duration_from_nanos
items.doc: Creates a `Duration` from a nanosecond count.

items.name: duration_from_micros
items.doc: Creates a `Duration` from a microsecond count.

items.name: duration_from_millis
items.doc: Creates a `Duration` from a millisecond count.

items.name: duration_from_secs
items.doc: Creates a `Duration` from a whole-second count.

items.name: as_nanos
items.doc: Returns the total nanoseconds in this duration.

items.name: as_micros
items.doc: Returns the total whole microseconds, truncated toward zero.

items.name: as_millis
items.doc: Returns the total whole milliseconds, truncated toward zero.

items.name: as_secs
items.doc: Returns the total whole seconds, truncated toward zero.

items.name: subsec_nanos
items.doc: Returns the sub-second nanosecond remainder; negative for negative durations.

items.name: is_zero
items.doc: Returns `true` if the duration is exactly zero.

items.name: is_negative
items.doc: Returns `true` if the duration is negative.

items.name: instant_now
items.doc:
Returns the current `Instant` from the monotonic clock (`CLOCK_MONOTONIC`).
`unsafe`: allocates a raw buffer for the libc `clock_gettime` call.

items.name: since
items.signature: fn since(self: Instant, earlier: Instant) -> Duration
items.doc:
Returns the elapsed time from `earlier` to `self` (`self - earlier`).
Negative if `earlier` is actually later; never panics (unlike Rust).

items.name: elapsed
items.doc: Returns the elapsed time since this `Instant` was captured.

items.name: nanos_since_epoch
items.doc: Returns the raw nanosecond reading; meaningful only relative to other `Instant`s.

items.name: system_time_now
items.doc:
Returns the current wall-clock time (`CLOCK_REALTIME`).
`unsafe`: allocates a raw buffer for the libc `clock_gettime` call.

items.name: unix_nanos
items.doc: Returns nanoseconds since the Unix epoch.

items.name: unix_secs
items.doc: Returns whole seconds since the Unix epoch, truncated toward zero.

items.name: unix_millis
items.doc: Returns whole milliseconds since the Unix epoch, truncated toward zero.

items.name: since
items.signature: fn since(self: SystemTime, earlier: SystemTime) -> Duration
items.doc: Returns the duration from `earlier` to `self` (`self - earlier`); may be negative.

items.name: sleep
items.doc:
Sleeps for the given duration via `nanosleep`; may wake early on a signal.
Returns immediately if `d` is zero or negative.

items.name: schema_catalog
items.doc:
Token-macro handler for `schema_catalog!{ S1, S2, … }` (see `resource` decls
like `resource cat = schema_catalog!{ Emp, Dept };` in `logos.std.deem` callers).
Reflects the named `schema` decls out of the trigger module's AST and emits a
`pub fn <name>() -> SchemaCatalog` returning a `SchemaCatalog::from_static` view
over a deduped `@{…}` Writ blob, for use by `Query::compile`/`Tpl::compile`.
Raises a diagnostic (via `error`) and returns an empty `ItemList` on a missing
binding name, an unknown/non-`schema` name, or a schema missing `code(0x…)`.

items.name: hashset_new
items.doc: Creates an empty `HashSet` with capacity 16.

items.name: hashset_with_capacity
items.doc: Creates an empty `HashSet` with `cap_hint` rounded up to a power of two (minimum 8).

items.name: btreemap_new
items.doc: Creates a new, empty `BTreeMap` with initial capacity 8.

items.name: btreemap_free
items.doc: Deallocates the `keys`/`vals` backing storage of `m`.

items.name: trama
items.doc:
Handler behind the `trama!{}` macro: compiles a template into a render fn.

Invocation form:
```text
resource render = trama!(c: &Ctx){ "template text" };
```
emits `pub fn render(c: &Ctx) -> Result<String, ElError>`. The parens carry a
real Logos parameter list, re-emitted verbatim into the generated signature;
the braces hold the template body, quoted (`"…"`) or backtick-delimited
(backticks avoid escaping embedded `"` string literals in EL expressions).

Template syntax: `{{ expr }}` interpolation; `{% for x in coll %}…{% endfor %}`;
`{% if cond %}…{% else %}…{% endif %}`; `{% set x = expr %}`. Struct-typed
params are reflected (ADR 0012 Phase 2) so field/loop-var types — including
nested loop element types — are inferred with no `with` clause needed.
Arithmetic errors in an embedded expression (overflow, div-by-zero) surface as
`Err(ElError)`, never a host trap. `name`/`params`/`s` are the raw macro
input: the binding name, the parameter-list text, and the raw brace body.

items.name: deem
items.doc:
Handler behind the `deem!{}` macro — Logos's Datalog/query DSL over `Writ` data. Invoked as `resource = deem!() { };`; `name` is the `resource` binding, `params` is the raw parenthesized fn-parameter-list source (slice params are query sources, scalar params are bare-referenced in clauses), and `body` is the raw `{ … }` query-block text. Splices a generated native fn named `name` back into the caller's module (this handler itself returns an empty `ItemList`); the generated fn's shape depends on the query's terminal clause: - `select : ` → `pub fn name() -> Result, ElError>`, one row per match (error-returning: overflow/div-by-zero in the EL expression is `Err`, never a host trap); - `from find

` → `pub fn name() -> Result, ElError>`, a borrow of the first row matching `P` (early-exit, zero-copy; `find` takes no other clause). Reports a compiler `error` (not a panic) and returns an empty `ItemList` on a missing binding name, an empty/malformed `params` list, or a query that fails to parse.

items.name: deem_walk
items.doc:
Implements the `wql_walk!` macro: `resource <name> = wql_walk!(<params>){ from … select … }`.
Parses `params` into `MacroParams`, parses `body` via the peg-generated surface parser into
an `RQuery` plan, then walks that plan with `plan_walker::walk_program_params` to emit the
native query fn as `name`.
On a missing `name`, empty `params`, or a malformed `body`, emits a compiler `error` and
returns an empty `ItemList` (no panic).

items.name: branchnode_subtree_size_shuttle
items.signature: fn branchnode_subtree_size_shuttle(br: *const BranchNode<K, STORE_CFG, CFG>, target: u64) -> ShuttleResult

items.name: RevIter
items.doc: Reversing adapter over a `DoubleEndedIterator`: `next` pulls from the source's back.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Underlying double-ended iterator.

items.name: _t_marker
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T` (zero-sized stand-in); value unused.

items.name: CopiedIter
items.doc: By-ref bridge: copies each `&T` yielded by `inner` into an owned `T`.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Underlying by-ref iterator.

items.name: _t_marker
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T` (zero-sized stand-in); value unused.

items.name: MapIter
items.doc: Lazy map adapter applying `f` to each item of `inner`.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: f
items.doc: Mapper invoked per item.

items.name: FilterIter
items.doc: Lazy filter adapter yielding only items for which `pred` returns `true`.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: pred
items.doc: Predicate selecting the yielded items.

items.name: ScanIter
items.doc: Stateful map adapter: `f` mutates `state` per item and yields its `Option<B>` result.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: state
items.doc: Mutable state threaded through `f`.

items.name: f
items.doc: Step function; its `None` results surface directly.

items.name: FuseIter
items.doc: Fusing adapter: after the source first returns `None`, every later call returns `None`.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: done
items.doc: Set at the first `None` from the source.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: FilterMapIter
items.doc: Combined filter+map adapter: yields `b` where `f` returns `Some(b)`, skips `None` results.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: f
items.doc: Mapper; items it maps to `None` are skipped.

items.name: TakeIter
items.doc: Truncating adapter yielding at most `remaining` more items.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: remaining
items.doc: Number of items still allowed through.

items.name: SkipIter
items.doc: Skipping adapter: drops the first `remaining` items, then yields the rest.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: remaining
items.doc: Number of items still to drop.

items.name: TakeWhileIter
items.doc: Prefix adapter: yields items while `pred` holds; the failing item is consumed and dropped.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: pred
items.doc: Predicate gating each item.

items.name: done
items.doc: Set once `pred` has failed; the adapter stays exhausted.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: SkipWhileIter
items.doc: Adapter dropping the longest prefix satisfying `pred`, then yielding everything after.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: pred
items.doc: Predicate selecting the dropped prefix.

items.name: primed
items.doc: Set once the prefix has been dropped.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: StepByIter
items.doc: Striding adapter yielding the first item, then every `step`-th item after it.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: step
items.doc: Stride between yielded items.

items.name: first
items.doc: True until the first item has been yielded.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: InspectIter
items.doc: Pass-through adapter calling `visit` on a copy of each item.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: visit
items.doc: Observer invoked per item.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: PeekableIter
items.doc: One-item-lookahead wrapper: `peek` caches the next item without consuming it.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: peeked
items.doc: Cached lookahead item; `None` when the cache is empty.

items.name: IntersperseIter
items.doc: Separating adapter: yields `a, sep, b, sep, c, …`; empty/single sources emit no separator.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: sep
items.doc: Separator re-emitted (by raw copy) between consecutive items.

items.name: has_peeked
items.doc: True when `peeked_val` holds a queued item.

items.name: peeked_val
items.doc: One-item lookahead buffer (two-state form; see note above).

items.name: emit_sep
items.doc: True when the next yield must be the separator.

items.name: FlattenIter
items.doc: Flattening adapter: yields the elements of each inner iterator produced by `outer`, in order.

items.name: outer
items.doc: Outer iterator producing inner iterators.

items.name: has_cur
items.doc: True when `cur` holds a live inner iterator.

items.name: cur
items.doc: Currently-draining inner iterator.

items.name: ArrayChunksIter
items.doc: Chunking adapter grouping items into `[T; N]` arrays; a trailing partial chunk is dropped.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: buf
items.doc: Accumulation buffer for the chunk in progress.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: MapWindowsIter
items.doc:
Sliding-window adapter yielding `f` over each length-`N` window of the source.
Yields nothing if the source has fewer than `N` items.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: buf
items.doc: Current window contents.

items.name: f
items.doc: Mapper applied to a copy of each window.

items.name: primed
items.doc: True once the initial `N`-item fill has happened.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: _r
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `R`; value unused.

items.name: FlatMapIter
items.doc: Map-then-flatten adapter: `f` maps each outer item to an iterator whose elements are yielded.

items.name: outer
items.doc: Outer source iterator.

items.name: f
items.doc: Mapper producing an inner iterator per outer item.

items.name: has_cur
items.doc: True when `cur` holds a live inner iterator.

items.name: cur
items.doc: Currently-draining inner iterator.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: _u
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `U`; value unused.

items.name: ByRefIter
items.doc: Borrowing wrapper forwarding `next` to `*p`, so the original iterator keeps its position.

items.name: p
items.doc: Raw pointer to the borrowed source iterator.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: OnceIter
items.doc: Single-item source: yields `val` once, then `None` forever.

items.name: consumed
items.doc: Set after the single yield.

items.name: val
items.doc: Value copied out on the first `next`.

items.name: OptionIter
items.doc: 0-or-1-item source over an `Option<T>`; backs `IntoIterator` for `Option`.

items.name: state
items.doc: Remaining item, taken by the first `next`.

items.name: ResultIter
items.doc: 0-or-1-item source over a `Result`'s `Ok` value; an `Err` yields nothing (error dropped).

items.name: state
items.doc: Remaining item, taken by the first `next`.

items.name: EmptyIter
items.doc: Always-empty source: every `next` returns `None`.

items.name: RepeatIter
items.doc: Infinite source yielding a copy of `val` on every `next`.

items.name: CycleIter
items.doc:
Repeating adapter: when `active` exhausts, re-clones `orig` and continues forever.
An empty source just keeps yielding `None` (no infinite loop).

items.name: orig
items.doc: Pristine copy the cycle re-clones from.

items.name: active
items.doc: Currently-draining copy.

items.name: _t
items.doc: Phantom anchor for `T`; value unused.

items.name: EnumPair
items.doc: Index/value pair yielded by `EnumIter`.

items.name: idx
items.doc: Zero-based position of `val` in the source sequence.

items.name: val
items.doc: The item itself.

items.name: EnumIter
items.doc: Enumerating adapter pairing each item with its zero-based index.

items.name: inner
items.doc: Source iterator.

items.name: idx
items.doc: Index the next front item will receive.

items.name: ChainIter
items.doc: Concatenating adapter: yields all of `first`, then all of `second`.

items.name: first
items.doc: First source, drained before `second`.

items.name: second
items.doc: Second source.

items.name: first_done
items.doc: Set once `first` is exhausted from the front.

items.name: ZipPair
items.doc: Item pair yielded by `ZipIter`.

items.name: first
items.doc: Item pulled from the left iterator.

items.name: second
items.doc: Item pulled from the right iterator.

items.name: ZipIter
items.doc:
Pairing adapter yielding `ZipPair`s until either side exhausts.
A left item pulled when the right side is already exhausted is dropped.

items.name: left
items.doc: Left source.

items.name: right
items.doc: Right source.

items.name: SliceIter
items.doc: Borrowing iterator over a slice, yielding `&T`; double-ended via distinct front/back cursors.

items.name: FromFnIter
items.doc: Closure-driven source: each `next` returns `f()`.

items.name: f
